schools.summary: The crowds press to hear the word; the lake of Gennesaret (= sea of Galilee/Tiberias, Bede); Christ withdraws into a ship to teach (Matt 13:2), fishing for those on shore.
Bede/Augustine read the ships as circumcision and uncircumcision, the fishermen as the Church's doctors, Simon's ship as the one primitive Church from which Christ teaches the nations.

Small fishing boats (a diminutive; John 6:23), belonging to Jonas's and Zebedee's families; 'washing' (not 'mending' as in Matthew/Mark) implies they had given up after a fruitless night and were cleaning the nets to lay them up.

Christ asks (gently) to use Simon's boat as a pulpit, putting out from the press of the crowd; he 'sat down', the Jewish teaching posture (Matt 5:1); the still cove carried his voice to the amphitheatre of the shore.
Augustine: putting out 'a little' teaches moderation — the multitude neither taught mere earthly things nor rushed into the depths of the sacraments.

Christ rewards the lender with a double kindness — fish and discipleship; Ambrose: 'launch into the deep' = the deep knowledge of the Son of God, the nets the apostles' words that save the caught; a figure of the Gentile harvest (Cyril), distinct from John 21:6 (Augustine).
Having taught, Christ turns to his mighty works, calling the fishermen by their own art; the command prefaces both the miracle and Peter's call.

'Master' (epistatēs, peculiar to Luke) recognizes authority, less than 'Rabbi/teacher', yet implies prior discipleship (John 21:3); Peter's weary candour after a fruitless night; obedient faith — 'at thy word' against his own trade-knowledge (Ps 127:1).
Calvin: Peter esteemed Christ and respected his authority, beginning anew what he had vainly attempted, though his faith was as yet slender.
A great multitude enclosed at one cast; 'net brake' = was beginning to break / straining (the imperfect), not actually torn open or the fish would escape — so Luke does not contradict John 21.
Calvin: the miracle's design was to manifest Christ's divinity and draw disciples; labour under Christ's guidance is never without God's blessing (Eccl 11:6).
They beckon (too far to call, or struck dumb with astonishment) to their partners (metochoi, business company; koinōnoi in v.10, Phil 4:3); both boats filled and beginning to sink (inchoative).
Augustine/Bede: the second ship = Judea or the Gentile Church, both filled, for every knee shall bow, Jew and Greek.
Clarke laments the envious separation of sects — only by Christian fellowship and mutual help is the great catch saved (Gal 6:2).
The miracle convicts Peter, who falls in awe; 'depart from me' is humility and consciousness of unworthiness (Isa 6:5), not dislike — the right response of a sinner before divine presence.

Cyril: Peter, learning from the law to distinguish defiled and holy, trembles as unclean; Ambrose: confess sin and the Lord pardons; Augustine reads the carnal Church pushing away the good; Bede: the Lord allays such fear so none dread the path of holiness.

themes: depart, I am a sinful man

schools.summary: Astonishment seized Peter and all with him (Andrew, the servants) — 'a wonder held him round' — at a catch beyond all natural explanation after a fruitless night.
Gregory of Nyssa: the voice of the Word is the voice of power, at whose bidding the creatures came forth (Ps 33:9) — the same Word now summons the fish.

themes: the voice of the creating Word

schools.summary: James and John, Simon's partners (koinōnoi, Phlm 17); 'fear not... catch men' (Luke's form of Matt 4:19); the verb zōgreō = catch alive, for life not death (2 Tim 2:26) — fulfilled at Pentecost.
Calvin: a special call to apostolic office, not mere faith; Christ chose rough unlearned men to renew them by his Spirit and humble the pride of the flesh.

They forsook all — little, but their whole living — showing love and self-denial (Phil 3:7); prompt obedience; harmonized with the earlier call (John 1:43) by a temporary return to fishing before this decisive break.
Chrysostom: faith and obedience that delayed not; Augustine: the forsaking figures the end of time, when Christ's own depart from the world's storms.

The leper falls in worship (Athanasius: adoring the Creator in a created temple); humble faith — 'if thou wilt' doubts not his mercy but owns his unworthiness (Ambrose), committing all to God's will (Titus); Cyril: 'thou canst' confesses divine power, same power = same substance.
A man 'full of leprosy' (Matt 8:2; Lev 13:45) at its worst; no place named, signifying all nations healed; a model suppliant submitting his desire to Christ's will.

themes: fell on his face in worship

schools.summary: Christ touches the leper though the law forbade it — Lord of the law, fearless of contagion, his flesh life-giving (Theophylact); 'I will... be clean' answers the request and reveals the two natures (Ambrose vs Photinus/Arius/Manichaeus; Greg Nyssa: soul wills, body touches).
He touched in compassion; the leprosy departed immediately and wholly, as Naaman's flesh was restored (2 Kings 5:14) — nothing between his command and the work.

themes: Lord of the law, fearless of contagion

schools.summary: The cleansed leper sent to the priest for the certificate of cleansing per Moses (Lev 14:2-32), like release from quarantine; 'a testimony unto them' (Matt 8:4) — living proof to priests and people of a Healer of leprosy.
Ambrose: the silence teaches good deeds concealed, avoiding even favour; Chrysostom: keep God before the eyes and give him glory after the cure; Cyril: the deed published itself.

The charge of silence had the opposite effect — the grateful man spread it, and the fame kept going (imperfects of continuous action); great multitudes came to hear and be healed (Matt 4:25), the swelling tide of Galilean popularity.

themes: to hear and to be healed

schools.summary: Christ habitually withdrew (hypochōreō, only in Luke; periphrastic imperfect) into the desert places and prayed — 'prayed' peculiar to Luke, who stresses Christ's prayer-life; at the height of popularity he sought solitude with the Father (Mark 1:35; Matt 14:23).
Clarke: the example for ministers — receive fresh power by prayer; little good is done when preachers keep in the crowd and are seldom alone with God.

themes: the secret of power in prayer

schools.summary: Cyril: Christ heals by his own inherent power, not borrowed; the miracle performed to attest his power before the slighting scribes; Bede: he first looses the chains of sin, the palsy being its punishment.
Pharisees and doctors gathered from every town to watch as he taught; the power of the Lord present to heal (Mark 2:1; Matt 9:2).

themes: the power of the Lord to heal

schools.summary: Four men bring the helpless paralytic (Mark 2:3); Luke's 'paralysed' (paralelumenos) the technical medical term (Hippocrates/Galen) vs Mark's vernacular; 'they sought' a conative imperfect — persistent intercessory effort.

Barred by the crowd, they reach the flat roof by the outside stairway (Acts 10:9) and let the pallet (klinidion, a small couch) down through the tiles (Mark 2:4) — a bold, inventive faith over every obstacle.
Chrysostom: they are to be admired for attempting a new and untried way when the door was shut.

themes: the flat roof and the pallet

schools.summary: 'Their faith' — of bearers and sufferer alike (the man would not be let down unless he believed); 'Man' (Luke) for 'Son' (Mark/Matthew); pardon granted before the cure.

Ambrose: God pardons one for another's good deed — bring the Church's prayers; Bede: the palsy was the punishment of sin, loosed first (John 5:14); Augustine: forgiven as man, that the forgiver be known as God.

Bede/Ambrose: the scribes' premise — only God forgives (Isa 43:25; Ps 103:3) — is true; the error is not seeing Christ forgives as God; the madness of confessing it yet not believing God when he forgives.
Clarke: were Jesus not God this would be blasphemy; he works three miracles (pardon, reading thoughts, the cure) as full proof of his Godhead.

debates.question: Was the scribes' reasoning ('only God forgives') correct?

sides.position: The premise is true; their error was not seeing that Christ is God — the truth condemns them out of their own mouths

themes: condemned out of their own mouths

schools.summary: Christ perceives their secret 'reasonings' as God omniscient (John 2:25; Mark 2:8) — itself the second proof of his Godhead, since to read the heart is God's prerogative; the objection becomes its own refutation.
Chrysostom: remove the fountain of evil and the waters of sickness cease — the deeper cure is of the heart.

Gill: Christ turns the objection back — both 'thy sins are forgiven' and 'rise and walk' belong to God alone; the forgiveness is the 'easier' to say because unverifiable, so he does the visible, testable cure to vouch for the invisible pardon.

The cure given 'that ye may know' the Son of man's power to forgive (Mark 2:10); 'Son of man' asserts true man yet God manifest in flesh (Gill); the parenthesis ('he said to the palsied') alike in all three Gospels proves their interrelation; arise, take the couch, go home (John 5:8).

themes: the Son of man's power to forgive

schools.summary: The cure immediate and complete — he rose before them all, carried the bed that had carried him, and went home; 'glorifying God' done by the man himself, peculiar to Luke (Mark 2:12) — the visible miracle vindicating the pardon.

The common people (not the Pharisees) all amazed (ekstasis, beside themselves), glorifying God and filled with fear (Acts 4:21); 'strange things' (paradoxa, beyond expectation, peculiar to Luke) — sins forgiven and a miracle wrought.

themes: the people contrasted with the scribes

schools.summary: Levi = Matthew (Augustine); Bede: he accuses himself by name that none despair (1 Tim 1:13); Cyril: a rapacious publican snatched by grace, 'follow me' with the soul's affections; Chrysostom: instant obedience, the power of the caller; Basil: he risked the unfinished accounts.
The publican called from the receipt of custom (Matt 9:9); grace reaching the most despised calling, who rises at once leaving gain and security.

'He left all' (a detail in Luke alone) — office, business, profits; 'followed' an imperfect (continually, inchoative); the preacher must have no earthly entanglement (Clarke); counting all loss for Christ (Phil 3:8).
Calvin: the forsaking, like Paul's soldier (2 Tim 2:4), means wholehearted devotion, not literal desertion of house and property.

Levi's great feast (dochē, a reception) in gratitude and to bring his fellow publicans to Christ; Matthew omits it of himself (true religion seeks no praise, Prov 27:2); Luke the Gentile says 'others', not 'sinners'; Christ reclines with the motley crew (Matt 11:19).

The uninvited Pharisees and their scribes (Mark 2:16) murmur (egoggyzon, onomatopoetic) from the sidelines; the charge aimed through the disciples at the Master (Luke 15:2); Luke gives 'sinners' as actually spoken.
Ambrose: the murmur is the voice of the Devil, echoing the serpent's first word to Eve, diffusing their father's poison.
Christ vindicates himself and the disciples by the physician proverb; Luke's 'in health' (hygiainontes) the medical term vs Mark/Matthew's 'strong' (Matt 9:12; Jer 8:22).
Chrysostom: he reminds them they too are sick and he the Physician, who must touch the afflicted to cure — so must go among sinners.

Christ came to call sinners (1 Tim 1:15); 'to repentance' added by Luke alone — not that they remain sinners but be converted (Luke 15:7); Jesus accepts the Pharisaic division for the moment to score them.

Augustine: 'to repentance' guards against thinking sinners loved as sinners — called to be saved from iniquity as from sickness; Ambrose: 'the righteous' = those who boast of the law and seek not gospel grace, for none is justified by law but redeemed by grace.

themes: saved from sin as from sickness

schools.summary: Augustine: two fasts — of tribulation (for sin) and of joy (feeding on spiritual things); Cyril: fasting quells passions, a need not pressing on Christ's joyful company; the accounts harmonized (the questioners came themselves, Matt 9:14).
The critics press a new charge — Christ's disciples do not fast as John's and the Pharisees' do — assuming piety must be austere.
The bridegroom's presence makes a time of joy, not sorrow; the emphatic question expects 'no' (Matt 9:15) — the disciples' not fasting is the fitting gladness of a wedding.

Cyril: Christ's coming a great festival, uniting our nature to him as bride (John 3:29); the children of the bridechamber called by the new discipline; Ambrose/Basil: we who feast on Christ cannot fast, but live on every word of God.

The bridegroom 'taken away' = the passion (Augustine; John 16:20; Isa 22:12); then the fast of mortification and mourning (Bede); Ambrose: yet Christ is with us always — none takes him away unless we take ourselves from him.
Gill: the days came in a very little time; then they would fast — fasting reserved for its proper season of sorrow.

themes: none takes Christ unless we leave him

schools.summary: The third parable (only Luke calls it that); the better text — rending a piece from a new garment to patch an old — heightens the folly: it rends the new and misfits the old (Matt 9:16); 'rendeth' (schisas, whence 'schism').
Ambrose: fasting is the old garment to be put off (Col 3:9); the actions of the old and new man are not to be mixed.

themes: the gospel a whole new garment
old and new man not mixed

schools.summary: The 'bottles' are goatskins; new fermenting wine bursts old brittle skins, losing both; the old skins = the rigid Pharisees, unable to hold the new wine of gospel grace (Job 32:19).

New wine requires new skins ('must be put', blēteon, a literary verbal form only here in the NT); the new bottles = hearts renewed by the Spirit, filled with the joy of the gospel; both preserved together.

Peculiar to Luke; 'the old is good' (chrēstoteros, mild/agreeable); the old wine = the former (Pharisaic-legal) religion, the new = the gospel's freer life; men accustomed to the old cling to it — spoken with tolerant pity for the prejudice of custom (Jer 6:16).
